content:
| Property | Value | Relevance to Laser Cleaning |
|---|---|---|
| Melting Point | 950-1080 °C (depending on composition) |  Crucial for determining the maximum permissible temperature during laser cleaning to avoid melting or phase transformations.  Exceeding this temperature can lead to irreversible damage and alteration of the bronze's microstructure. |
| Thermal Conductivity | 110-120 W/m·K | Influences the rate of heat diffusion away from the laser interaction zone. Higher thermal conductivity facilitates rapid cooling, minimizing heat-affected zones and reducing the risk of thermal damage.  Precise control of fluence is vital to manage heat dissipation effectively. |
| Specific Heat Capacity | 385 J/kg·K | Determines the amount of energy required to raise the temperature of the bronze.  Understanding this value is critical for precise energy dosing to achieve efficient cleaning without causing excessive heating. |
| Optical Absorption Coefficient (at 1064 nm) | 10<sup>4</sup> - 10<sup>5</sup> cm<sup>-1</sup> (highly dependent on composition and surface condition) |  Dictates the depth of light penetration and hence the extent of material removal. Higher absorption leads to more efficient ablation but increases the risk of deeper damage if not carefully controlled. Wavelength selection is crucial for optimizing absorption and minimizing damage. |
| Surface Reflectivity (at 1064 nm) | 5-20% (highly dependent on surface finish and oxidation) | High reflectivity can reduce cleaning efficiency; appropriate pre-treatment or wavelength selection may be necessary to enhance absorption.  Surface roughness also influences reflectivity, affecting the uniformity of cleaning. |
| Hardness (Brinell) | 60-150 HB (depending on composition and alloying elements) |  Influences the ease of material removal and the susceptibility to surface damage during the cleaning process.  Lower hardness may require lower fluence to avoid excessive material removal. |
| Density | 8.7-8.9 g/cm³ |  Relevant for calculating the mass removed during ablation and for estimating the energy required for cleaning a specific area. |
| Oxide Layer Thickness | Variable (typically several micrometers) |  The presence of an oxide layer can influence laser absorption and ablation dynamics. Pre-cleaning or selection of a wavelength that effectively interacts with the oxide layer might be necessary.  Techniques such as Q-switched Nd:YAG lasers are frequently employed in such cases. |
| Ablation Threshold (at 1064 nm) | 1-5 J/cm² (highly dependent on pulse duration, wavelength, and surface condition) |  Represents the minimum fluence required to initiate material removal.  Operating above this threshold ensures efficient cleaning, while staying close to it minimizes damage to the underlying substrate.  Precise control of fluence is critical for achieving selective ablation. |
